parentQuestion: ALL preganglionic neurons in BOTH sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions release ___.

options: A neuron that comes after the ganglion
A neuron that extends from the CNS to the ganglion
A neuron in the brain only
scaffolds.explain: A PREGANGLIONIC neuron extends FROM the CNS (brain/spinal cord) TO a ganglion. 'Pre' = before. It's the first neuron in the two-neuron autonomic pathway, synapsing in the ganglion.

wrongExplains.0: That describes a POSTganglionic neuron. 'Pre' means before the ganglion, not after.
wrongExplains.2: Preganglionic neurons are motor/efferent neurons carrying commands OUT, not sensory neurons bringing info IN.
wrongExplains.3: Preganglionic neurons are in the spinal cord (sympathetic) AND brainstem (parasympathetic), not just the brain.

options: A cluster of neuron cell bodies outside the CNS
scaffolds.explain: A GANGLION is a cluster of neuron cell bodies located OUTSIDE the CNS. In the ANS, ganglia serve as relay stations where preganglionic neurons synapse with postganglionic neurons.

wrongExplains.0: Muscle fibers are effectors at the end of the pathway. Ganglia are neural relay stations.
wrongExplains.2: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers. A ganglion is a structural cluster of nerve cell bodies.
wrongExplains.3: Ganglia are specifically OUTSIDE the CNS. Clusters of cell bodies inside the CNS are called 'nuclei,' not ganglia.

scaffolds.q: What is the most common neurotransmitter used throughout the nervous system?
scaffolds.explain: ACETYLCHOLINE (ACh) is the most widespread neurotransmitter. It's used at neuromuscular junctions, in the brain, and throughout the ANS — at ALL preganglionic synapses and at parasympathetic target organs.

wrongExplains.0: Dopamine is important for movement and reward, but ACh is more widespread throughout the body.
wrongExplains.2: Serotonin is important for mood and gut function, but ACh has more total uses in the nervous system.
wrongExplains.3: GABA is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brain, but ACh is more widespread overall.

scaffolds.q: ALL preganglionic neurons (sympathetic AND parasympathetic) release which neurotransmitter?
scaffolds.explain: ALL preganglionic neurons release ACETYLCHOLINE — both sympathetic and parasympathetic. This is a universal rule. The difference between the divisions appears at the postganglionic level.

wrongExplains.0: Norepinephrine is released by sympathetic POSTganglionic neurons, not preganglionic.
wrongExplains.2: Epinephrine is primarily a hormone from the adrenal medulla, not a preganglionic neurotransmitter.
wrongExplains.3: Dopamine is used in the brain and some peripheral areas, but not at autonomic preganglionic synapses.

scaffolds.q: Neurons that release acetylcholine are called ___.
scaffolds.explain: CHOLINERGIC neurons release acetylCHOLINE. All preganglionic neurons are cholinergic. The suffix '-ergic' means 'working by' or 'releasing' that neurotransmitter.

wrongExplains.0: Adrenergic neurons release adrenaline/norepinephrine. Sympathetic postganglionic neurons are adrenergic.
wrongExplains.2: Dopaminergic neurons release dopamine. They're found in the brain, not at ANS ganglia.
wrongExplains.3: Serotonergic neurons release serotonin. Different neurotransmitter system entirely.

scaffolds.q: What type of receptor does ACh bind to in autonomic ganglia?
scaffolds.explain: In ganglia, ACh binds to NICOTINIC receptors on postganglionic neurons. These are fast-acting ion channel receptors (named because nicotine also activates them).

wrongExplains.0: Adrenergic receptors bind norepinephrine/epinephrine, not acetylcholine.
wrongExplains.2: GABA receptors bind GABA, an inhibitory neurotransmitter. ACh uses its own receptor types.
wrongExplains.3: Dopamine receptors bind dopamine. ACh binds to cholinergic receptors (nicotinic or muscarinic).

scaffolds.q: Why is it important that ALL preganglionic neurons use ACh?
options: It allows drugs to affect both divisions at the ganglionic level
It proves the two divisions are identical
ACh is the only neurotransmitter that exists
scaffolds.explain: This shared feature means GANGLIONIC BLOCKING DRUGS affect BOTH divisions simultaneously. A drug that blocks nicotinic receptors in ganglia will inhibit both sympathetic and parasympathetic transmission.

wrongExplains.0: It makes a big difference pharmacologically — drugs can target the common ganglionic synapse.
wrongExplains.2: The divisions differ at the postganglionic level, but share ACh at the preganglionic level.
wrongExplains.3: Many neurotransmitters exist. ACh happens to be the universal choice for preganglionic neurons.

scaffolds.explain: Parasympathetic postganglionic neurons ALSO release ACh. So parasympathetic is 'ACh all the way' — both preganglionic AND postganglionic neurons are cholinergic.

wrongExplains.0: Norepinephrine is released by SYMPATHETIC postganglionic neurons, not parasympathetic.
wrongExplains.2: Epinephrine is a hormone from the adrenal medulla, not a parasympathetic neurotransmitter.
wrongExplains.3: Cortisol is a stress hormone from the adrenal cortex, not a neurotransmitter.

scaffolds.explain: Most sympathetic postganglionic neurons release NOREPINEPHRINE (NE). This is the key difference: Sympathetic switches from ACh (preganglionic) to NE (postganglionic). One exception: Sweat glands use ACh.

wrongExplains.0: ACh is used by sympathetic PREganglionic neurons and to sweat glands, but most postganglionic use NE.
wrongExplains.2: Serotonin is primarily a CNS neurotransmitter and gut signaling molecule, not used by sympathetic postganglionic neurons.
wrongExplains.3: Histamine is involved in immune/allergic responses, not standard sympathetic neurotransmission.
